title: TPU v1 · Security Architecture for a Data-Center Inference ASIC: Root of Trust to Isolation
oneLine: This case explains how a server-attached inference ASIC can establish trust from power-on through runtime and isolate each tenant's models and data. Because TPU v1 publications do not emphasize security, the mechanisms shown are standard practice; published host-driven, Unified Buffer, and DDR3 facts provide context.

legend.name: ① Root of Trust (RoT)
legend.desc: The first immutable anchor: mask-programmed logic or one-time fuses containing a verification public key. Every later trust decision derives from it. This is standard data-center ASIC practice, not a published v1 detail.

legend.name: ② Secure boot / firmware integrity
legend.desc: At power-on, the RoT verifies the first firmware stage before execution; each verified stage authenticates the next. Any invalid signature halts startup so malicious firmware cannot gain control.

legend.name: ③ Tenant isolation boundary
legend.desc: When tenants or tasks reuse an accelerator, one user's weights and intermediates must remain inaccessible to another. Partitioning, access checks, and clearing before reuse establish the boundary. This is a general host-driven ASIC isolation model.

legend.name: ④ Storage isolation and clearing
legend.desc: The 24 MiB Unified Buffer and 8 GiB DDR3 are shared across tasks. Clear residual data and enforce address bounds when switching tenants to prevent weight or activation leakage.

legend.name: ⑤ Fail closed
legend.desc: The defining security rule is to deny or halt on verification failure, never continue in a degraded state. No stage receives control unless the preceding stage authenticates it.

steps.cap: ① Anchor trust in immutable mask logic or fuses containing the public key.
② Perform secure boot and staged firmware verification: authenticate before execution, measure each stage, and prevent rollback.
③ If any signature check fails, halt and deny release—fail closed.
④ After every stage verifies, enter a known-good runtime state and begin inference.
⑤ Under multi-tenancy, partition UB/DDR3, check access bounds, and clear state on switches to isolate each user's models and data.

before: Define the threat model: A data-center accelerator must resist firmware replacement, malicious code in the boot chain, and cross-tenant leakage of model weights or data. TPU v1 publications do not detail security, so use standard inference-ASIC threats.

Decisions to make: RoT implementation (mask logic or fuses), signature and measurement schemes, chain depth, anti-rollback policy, tenant isolation granularity, and partitioning/clearing of the UB and DDR3.
Required inputs: The host-driven operating model with PCIe-submitted CISC commands, shared resources such as the 24 MiB UB and 8 GiB DDR3, and cloud multi-tenancy and compliance requirements.

Disclosure boundary: Public v1 sources reveal almost no security mechanism. RoT, secure boot, and isolation are explicitly standard data-center inference-ASIC practice; only published host-driven, UB, and DDR3 facts are used as context.
during: Anchor trust in immutable hardware: The RoT must be unmodifiable by software, using mask logic or one-time fuses, and hold the verification public key. Otherwise the trust chain has no stable starting point.

Verify before execution: The RoT authenticates first-stage firmware; only then may that stage run and verify the next. More verification improves assurance but adds startup latency and logic, so chain depth follows the threat model.
Fail closed and prevent rollback: Any failed check halts startup. Monotonic version state must also prevent installation of older firmware with known vulnerabilities.

Choose the isolation model: Host-driven v1-style batch operation can isolate most simply by serving one task at a time and clearing state between tasks. Concurrent tenants require hardware UB/DDR3 partitions and bounds checks at greater area and complexity.
Protect shared storage: Clear residual weights and activations in the 24 MiB UB and off-chip DDR3 before reuse, and validate address ranges to prevent one tenant from reading another's state.

Balance security with performance: Signature checks, clearing, and isolation cost latency, area, and power within a 28–40 W envelope. Put most overhead in startup and task switching so steady-state throughput remains unaffected.

result.label: Root of Trust
result.value: Mask/fuse-anchored public key (standard practice; not disclosed for v1)

result.label: Secure boot
result.value: Authenticate each stage before execution; halt on failure

result.label: Tenant isolation
result.value: Partition / bounds-check / clear on switch (general practice)

result.label: Shared storage
result.value: Clear 24 MiB UB + 8 GiB DDR3 before reuse

result.label: TPU v1 disclosure
result.value: Security not emphasized; mechanisms here are qualitative practice

after: Test that the chain breaks safely: Corrupt firmware and attempt rollback, verifying that hardware denies release or halts rather than continuing in a compromised state.

Test that isolation does not leak: After task or tenant switches, verify UB and DDR3 clearing and attempt cross-boundary reads. There are no public v1 isolation metrics, so derive tests from standard ASIC practice.

Propagate requirements: Give physical design layout and isolation rules for RoT, fuses, and key storage; give firmware and the TensorFlow/XLA stack signing, provisioning, and anti-rollback procedures.

Reconcile performance and power: Feed security latency and area into §3.9, ensuring the 28–40 W budget and steady-state throughput remain intact. Most security cost should occur at boot or task switches.

Document the boundary: State that RoT, secure boot, and tenant isolation are standard data-center inference-ASIC practices, not disclosed TPU v1 features; cite host-driven operation, UB, and DDR3 only as published context.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the paper does not emphasize security; RoT, secure boot, firmware verification, and tenant isolation are described as standard data-center inference-ASIC practice, not published v1 details.)
